features.summary: Chevron delivered strong Q2 2022 results with ROCE over 25%, the highest since 2008. Adjusted earnings increased $8 billion year-over-year driven by higher upstream realizations and downstream refining margins. The company returned over $5 billion to shareholders through dividends and buybacks while increasing total investment to $6.8 billion including the REG acquisition. Production decreased 7% year-over-year due to contract expirations in Indonesia and Thailand, partially offset by Permian growth.
features.management_outlook: Management raised the top end of share buyback guidance to $15 billion annually, expecting to maintain this rate across commodity cycles. Capital expenditure guidance remains $15-17 billion through 2026 with room to increase within this range. The company expects 3% upstream compound annual growth through 2026 while maintaining capital discipline. TCO's WPMP startup is expected in H2 2023 with FGP timing unchanged for H1 2024.
features.risks: Key risks include potential CPC pipeline disruptions affecting TCO exports, though management noted current full capacity operations. Planned turnarounds in Q3 will reduce production across multiple locations. Commodity price volatility remains a concern, though the company emphasizes cycle-resilient operations. Inflation pressures continue in the single digits for onshore US operations, with management working to secure necessary goods and services for 2023.
features.key_drivers: Higher commodity realizations and refining margins drove strong financial performance. Permian production growth continues with record levels and improved capital efficiency, with development costs down 25% since 2019. The REG acquisition and Bunge joint venture expand renewable fuels capabilities toward 100,000 barrels per day target. LNG agreements for 4 million tons annually from US Gulf Coast provide new Atlantic Basin market access starting 2026.
